revision.text: #redirect[[en:Suika_Shinto]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''垂加神道'''（'''すいかしんとう'''・'''しでますしんとう'''）は江戸時代前期に提唱された神道説。[[吉川惟足]]から[[吉田神道]]を、[[度会延佳]]から[[伊勢神道]]を学んだ儒者・山崎闇斎が、それらを[[朱子学]]の理論によって統合したもの。
{{msg:stub}}

'''垂加神道'''（'''すいかしんとう'''・'''しでますしんとう'''）は江戸時代前期に提唱された神道説。[[吉川惟足]]から[[吉田神道]]を、[[度会延佳]]から[[伊勢神道]]を学んだ儒者・山崎闇斎が、それらを[[朱子学]]の理論によって統合したもの。
{{stub}}

'''垂加神道'''（'''すいかしんとう'''、'''しでますしんとう'''）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に提唱された[[神道]]説。
[[吉川惟足]]から[[吉田神道]]を、[[度会延佳]]から[[伊勢神道]]を学んだ儒者・[[山崎闇斎]]が、それらを[[朱子学]]の理論によって統合したものである。
{{stub}} [[category:神道|すいかしんとう]]

'''垂加神道'''（'''すいかしんとう'''、'''しでますしんとう'''）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に提唱された[[神道]]説。
[[吉川惟足]]から[[吉田神道]]を、[[度会延佳]]から[[伊勢神道]]を学んだ儒者・[[山崎闇斎]]が、それらを[[朱子学]]の理論によって統合したものである。
{{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|すいかしんとう]]

'''垂加神道'''（'''すいかしんとう'''、'''しでますしんとう'''）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に提唱された[[神道]]説。
[[吉川惟足]]から[[吉田神道]]を、[[度会延佳]]から[[伊勢神道]]を学んだ儒者・[[山崎闇斎]]が、それらを[[朱子学]]の理論によって統合したものである。
{{shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道の流派|すいかしんとう]]

'''垂加神道'''（'''すいかしんとう'''、'''しでますしんとう'''）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に提唱された[[神道]]説。
[[吉川惟足]]から[[吉田神道]]を、[[度会延佳]]から[[伊勢神道]]を学んだ儒者・[[山崎闇斎]]が、それらを[[朱子学]]の理論によって統合したものである。神道は[[天照大神]]の徳を学ぶことであると説き、[[天皇]]への信仰を示した。
{{shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道の流派|すいかしんとう]]

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] {{神道}}
'''垂加神道'''（'''すいかしんとう'''、'''しでますしんとう'''）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[山崎闇斎]]が提唱した[[神道]]説。[[吉川神道]]や[[伊勢神道]]と並ぶ儒家神道のひとつとされる<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 垂加神道は、吉川神道を始めとする神道の諸教説を学んだ山崎闇斎が、吉川神道の流れをさらに発展させ、[[朱子学]]、陰陽学、[[易経|易学]]をも取り入れた神道の集大成として完成させたもので、道徳性が強い内容となっている<ref name="yougo1" />。
=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳から伊勢神道を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、吉川神道の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である。
=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会|authorlink=|title=必携日本史用語|origdate=2009-2-2|accessdate=|edition=四訂版|publisher=[[実教出版]]|series=|isbn=9784407316599}}</ref>。また、人間の「敬」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。
== 脚注 == <references/>
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会|authorlink=|title=必携日本史用語|origdate=2009-2-2|accessdate=|edition=四訂版|publisher=[[実教出版]]|series=|isbn=9784407316599}}
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すいかしんとう}} [[Category:神道の流派|すいかしんとう]]

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] {{神道}}
'''垂加神道'''（'''すいかしんとう'''、'''しでますしんとう'''）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[山崎闇斎]]が提唱した[[神道]]説。[[吉川神道]]や[[伊勢神道]]と並ぶ儒家神道のひとつとされる<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 垂加神道は、吉川神道を始めとする神道の諸教説を学んだ山崎闇斎が、吉川神道の流れをさらに発展させ、[[朱子学]]、陰陽学、[[易経|易学]]をも取り入れた神道の集大成として完成させたもので、道徳性が強い内容となっている<ref name="yougo1" />。
=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳から伊勢神道を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、吉川神道の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である。
=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会|authorlink=|title=必携日本史用語|origdate=2009-2-2|accessdate=|edition=四訂版|publisher=[[実教出版]]|series=|isbn=9784407316599}}</ref>。また、人間の「敬」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。また、この系譜から[[竹内式部]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった。

== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会|authorlink=|title=必携日本史用語|origdate=2009-2-2|accessdate=|edition=四訂版|publisher=[[実教出版]]|series=|isbn=9784407316599}}
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すいかしんとう}} [[Category:神道の流派|すいかしんとう]]

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] {{神道}}
'''垂加神道'''（すいかしんとう、しでますしんとう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[山崎闇斎]]が提唱した[[神道]]説。[[吉川神道]]や[[伊勢神道]]と並ぶ[[儒家神道]]のひとつとされる<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}}</ref>。

=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会|authorlink=|title=必携日本史用語|origdate=2009-2-2|accessdate=|edition=四訂版|publisher=[[実教出版]]|series=|isbn=9784407316599}}</ref>。また、人間の「敬」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。また、この系譜から[[竹内式部]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった。

== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会|authorlink=|title=必携日本史用語|origdate=2009-2-2|accessdate=|edition=四訂版|publisher=[[実教出版]]|series=|isbn=9784407316599}}

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] {{神道}}
'''垂加神道'''（すいかしんとう、しでますしんとう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[山崎闇斎]]が提唱した[[神道]]説。[[吉川神道]]や[[伊勢神道]]と並ぶ[[儒家神道]]のひとつとされる<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会| title=日本史B用語集―A併記 |year=2009 |edition=改訂版 |publisher=山川出版社}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 垂加神道は、吉川神道を始めとする神道の諸教説を学んだ山崎闇斎が、吉川神道の流れをさらに発展させ、[[朱子学]]、陰陽学、[[易経|易学]]をも取り入れた神道の集大成として完成させたもので、道徳性が強い内容となっている<ref name="yougo1" />。
=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳から伊勢神道を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、吉川神道の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である。
=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |year=2009 |edition=四訂版 |publisher=実教出版}}</ref>。また、人間の「敬」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。この「敬」の実践行為とは「正直」であるとした<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |series=中公新書 |publisher=中央公論新社}}</ref>。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。また、この系譜から[[竹内式部]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった。

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] {{神道}}

== 概要 == 垂加神道は、吉川神道を始めとする神道の諸教説を学んだ山崎闇斎が、吉川神道の流れをさらに発展させ、[[朱子学]]、陰陽学、[[易経|易学]]をも取り入れた神道の集大成として完成させたもので、道徳性が強い内容となっている<ref name="yougo1" />。
=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳から伊勢神道を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、吉川神道の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。
=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |year=2009 |edition=四訂版 |publisher=実教出版}}</ref>。また、人間の「敬」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。この「敬」の実践行為とは「正直」であるとした<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |series=中公新書 |publisher=中央公論新社}}</ref>。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。また、この系譜から[[竹内敬持|竹内式部]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絅斎|浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。
== 脚注 == <references/>
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |series=[[中公新書]] |publisher=[[中央公論新社]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会| title=日本史B用語集―A併記 |year=2009 |edition=改訂版 |publisher=[[山川出版社]] |isbn=978-4-634-01302-5}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |year=2009 |edition=四訂版 |publisher=[[実教出版]] |isbn=978-4-407-31659-9}}
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すいかしんとう}} [[Category:神道の流派]] [[Category:江戸時代の神道]]

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] {{神道}}
'''垂加神道'''（すいかしんとう、しでますしんとう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[山崎闇斎]]が提唱した[[神道]]説。[[吉川神道]]や[[伊勢神道]]と並ぶ[[儒家神道]]のひとつとされる<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会| title=日本史B用語集―A併記 |year=2009 |edition=改訂版 |publisher=山川出版社}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 垂加神道は、吉川神道を始めとする神道の諸教説を学んだ山崎闇斎が、吉川神道の流れをさらに発展させ、[[朱子学]]、陰陽学、[[易経|易学]]をも取り入れた神道の集大成として完成させたもので、道徳性が強い内容となっている<ref name="yougo1" />。
=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳から伊勢神道を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、吉川神道の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。「倭姫命世記」の「神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本」の語句に由来する。
=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |year=2009 |edition=四訂版 |publisher=実教出版}}</ref>。また、人間の「敬」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。この「敬」の実践行為とは「正直」であるとした<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |series=中公新書 |publisher=中央公論新社}}</ref>。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。また、この系譜から[[竹内敬持|竹内式部]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絅斎|浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。

=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳(出口延佳)及び大中臣精長(河辺精長)から[[伊勢神道]]を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、[[吉川神道]]の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。「[[神道五部書|倭姫命世記]]」の「神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本」の語句に由来する。

=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。また、この系譜から[[竹内敬持|竹内式部]]や[[山県大弐]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絅斎|浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。
== 脚注 == <references/>

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] '''垂加神道'''（すいかしんとう、しでますしんとう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[山崎闇斎]]が提唱した[[神道]]説。[[吉川神道]]や[[伊勢神道]]と並ぶ[[儒家神道]]のひとつとされる<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会| title=日本史B用語集―A併記 |year=2009 |edition=改訂版 |publisher=山川出版社}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 垂加神道は、吉川神道を始めとする神道の諸教説を学んだ山崎闇斎が、吉川神道の流れをさらに発展させ、[[朱子学]]、陰陽学、[[易経|易学]]をも取り入れた神道の集大成として完成させたもので、道徳性が強い内容となっている<ref name="yougo1" />。
=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳([[出口延佳]])及び大中臣精長([[河辺精長|河邊精長]])から[[伊勢神道]]を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、[[吉川神道]]の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。「[[神道五部書|倭姫命世記]]」の「神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本」の語句に由来する。
=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |year=2009 |edition=四訂版 |publisher=実教出版}}</ref>。また、人間の「敬」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。この「敬」の実践行為とは「正直」であるとした<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |series=中公新書 |publisher=中央公論新社}}</ref>。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。また、この系譜から[[竹内敬持|竹内式部]]や[[山県大弐]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絅斎|浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。
== 脚注 == <references/>
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |series=[[中公新書]] |publisher=[[中央公論新社]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会| title=日本史B用語集―A併記 |year=2009 |edition=改訂版 |publisher=[[山川出版社]] |isbn=978-4-634-01302-5}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |year=2009 |edition=四訂版 |publisher=[[実教出版]] |isbn=978-4-407-31659-9}}

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] '''垂加神道'''（すいかしんとう、しでますしんとう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[山崎闇斎]]が提唱した[[神道]]説。[[吉川神道]]や[[伊勢神道]]と並ぶ[[儒家神道]]のひとつとされる<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会| title=日本史B用語集―A併記 |year=2009 |edition=改訂版 |publisher=山川出版社}}</ref>。[[日本書紀]]の研究にも関係があったとされる<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=『日本書紀の誕生: 編纂と受容の歴史』(八木書店) - 編集：遠藤 慶太,河内 春人,関根 淳,細井 浩志 - 河内 春人による本文抜粋 | 好きな書評家、読ませる書評。|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18||accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 垂加神道は、[[吉川神道]]を始めとする神道の諸教説を学んだ山崎闇斎が、吉川神道の流れをさらに発展させ、[[朱子学]]、陰陽学、[[易経|易学]]をも取り入れた神道の集大成として完成させたもので、道徳性が強い内容となっている<ref name="yougo1" />。
=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳([[出口延佳]])及び大中臣精長([[河辺精長|河邊精長]])から[[伊勢神道]]を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、[[吉川神道]]の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。「[[神道五部書|倭姫命世記]]」の「神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本」の語句に由来する。
=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |year=2009 |edition=四訂版 |publisher=実教出版}}</ref>。また、人間の「敬」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。この「敬」の実践行為とは「正直」であるとした<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |series=中公新書 |publisher=中央公論新社}}</ref>。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた<ref name="yougo2" />。また、この系譜から[[竹内敬持|竹内式部]]や[[山県大弐]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絅斎|浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。
== 脚注 == <references/>
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡|authorlink=伊藤聡|title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |series=[[中公新書]] |publisher=[[中央公論新社]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会| title=日本史B用語集―A併記 |year=2009 |edition=改訂版 |publisher=[[山川出版社]] |isbn=978-4-634-01302-5}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |year=2009 |edition=四訂版 |publisher=[[実教出版]] |isbn=978-4-407-31659-9}}
== 関連項目 == *[[宝暦事件]] *[[明和事件]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すいかしんとう}} [[Category:神道の流派]] [[Category:江戸時代の神道]]

revision.text:
[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Goddess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]|isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]|isbn=978-4-634-01302-5}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]|isbn=978-4-407-31659-9}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hōreki Incident|en|宝暦事件}} * {{ill|Meiwa Incident|en|明和事件}}
{{Shinto}} [[Category:Schools of Shinto]] [[Category:Edo period Shinto]] [[ja:垂加神道]]

It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>

Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Goddess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[category:new pages]]ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]|isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]|isbn=978-4-634-01302-5}} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]|isbn=978-4-407-31659-9}}

== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />

He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").

It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

{{Shinto}} [[Category:Schools of Shinto]] [[Category:Edo period Shinto]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Suika Shinto]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Suika Shinto]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:垂加神道]]

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Suika Shinto-qq]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Suika Shinto-qq]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Suika Shinto-qq]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Suika Shinto-qq]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Suika Shinto-qq]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Suika Shinto-qq]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]]<!--Suika Shinto-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suika Shinto]][[Category:qq]] <!--Suika Shinto-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto'''[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suika Shinto]][[Category:qq]] <!--Suika Shinto-->ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[File:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Yamazaki Ansai]] '''Suika Shintō''' (垂加神道, Suika Shintō) is a Shintō doctrine proposed by [[Yamazaki Ansai]] in the early [[Edo period]]. It is considered one of the schools of [[Confucian Shintō]], alongside [[Yoshikawa Shintō]] and [[Ise Shintō]].<ref name="yougo1">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=National History Education Research Council|title=Japanese History B Terminology Collection―with A Notes|year=2009|edition=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Yamakawa Publishing]]}}</ref> It is also said to be related to studies of the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=The Birth of Nihon Shoki: The History of Compilation and Reception (Yagi Shoten) - Edited by Keita Endo, Haruhito Kawachi, Atsushi Sekine, Hiroshi Hosoi - Excerpts by Haruhito Kawachi|publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18|accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto''' [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suika Shinto]][[Category:qq]] <!--Suika Shinto-->ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

== Overview ==
Suika Shintō is a comprehensive form of Shintō developed by Yamazaki Ansai, who studied various Shintō doctrines, including Yoshikawa Shintō. He further refined these teachings by incorporating elements of [[Zhu Xi Confucianism]], [[Onmyōdō]], and [[I Ching|Yi Studies]], creating a doctrine deeply rooted in morality.<ref name="yougo1" />
=== Origin of the Name "Suika" === Originally a [[Rinzai school]] monk, Yamazaki Ansai later studied [[Confucianism]] but found it insufficient. He subsequently learned [[Ise Shintō]] from [[Watarai Nobuyoshi]] (also known as Deguchi Nobuyoshi) and [[Ōnakatomi no Masanaga]] (also known as Kawabe no Masanaga). Ansai later apprenticed under [[Yoshikawa Koretari]] and mastered the esoteric teachings of Yoshikawa Shintō. The name "Suika" was bestowed upon Ansai by Koretari at that time.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}} It originates from a phrase in the ''{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}'' that states, "神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本" ("The divine descends; prayer comes first; blessings arise from uprightness and sincerity").
=== Philosophy === Suika Shintō defines Shintō as the way in which the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], as a descendant of the [[Amaterasu|Sun Go'''Suika Shinto''' [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suika Shinto]][[Category:qq]] <!--Suika Shinto-->ess]], governs under her divine will. It emphasizes reverence for the Emperor and advocates the unification of Shintō and Confucianism, leading to the rise of {{ill|sonnō thought|en|尊王思想}} (reverence for the emperor).<ref name="yougo2">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Japanese History Terminology Study Group|title=Essential Japanese History Terms|year=2009|edition=4th Edition|publisher=[[Jikkyo Publishing]]}}</ref>
The philosophy places utmost importance on the human virtue of "reverence" (敬). Ansai taught that by fully embodying reverence, humans could achieve unity with heaven and earth ("the unity of heaven and man"). He defined "reverence in action" as "uprightness" (正直).<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Satoshi Itō|title=What is Shintō?|year=2012|series=[[Chūkō Shinsho]]|publisher=[[Chūōkōron-shinsha]]}}</ref>
=== Influence === Due to its emphasis on reverence for the Emperor, Suika Shintō became the ideological backbone of [[sonnō thought]] and greatly influenced the [[Mito School]]'s reverence for the Emperor, as well as [[nationalism]] and [[ultranationalism]].<ref name="yougo2" /> Figures such as {{ill|Takenouchi Keiji|en|竹内敬持}} and {{ill|Yamagata Daini|en|山県大弐}} emerged as fervent imperial loyalists from this tradition. Similarly, {{ill|Asami Keisai|en|浅見絅斎}} advocated for reverence for the Emperor through Confucian theories of moral obligation. These developments eventually contributed to the flourishing of {{ill|Nativism (Japan)|en|日本主義}} and [[kokugaku]] studies.{{citation needed|date=August 28, 2017}}

[[ファイル:Yamazaki Ansai.jpg|thumb|right|250px|山崎闇斎]] '''垂加神道'''（すいかしんとう、しでますしんとう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[山崎闇斎]]が提唱した[[神道]]説。[[吉川神道]]や[[伊勢神道]]と並ぶ[[儒家神道]]のひとつとされる{{Sfn|『日本史B用語集―A併記』}}。[[日本書紀]]の研究にも関係があったとされる<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://allreviews.jp/review/5126|title=『日本書紀の誕生: 編纂と受容の歴史』(八木書店) - 編集：遠藤 慶太,河内 春人,関根 淳,細井 浩志 - 河内 春人による本文抜粋 |publisher=ALL REVIEWS|date=2020-11-18||accessdate=2021-01-03}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 垂加神道は、[[吉川神道]]を始めとする神道の諸教説を学んだ山崎闇斎が、吉川神道の流れをさらに発展させ、[[朱子学]]、陰陽学、[[易経|易学]]をも取り入れた神道の集大成として完成させたもので、道徳性が強い内容となっている{{Sfn|『日本史B用語集―A併記』}}。
=== 「垂加」の由来 === [[臨済宗]]の僧侶であった山崎闇斎は、その後儒教を学ぶがあきたらず、度会延佳([[出口延佳]])及び大中臣精長([[河辺精長|河邊精長]])から[[伊勢神道]]を学び、ついで[[吉川惟足]]に師事し、[[吉川神道]]の奥義を伝授された。垂加とは、このとき、惟足が闇斎に贈った号である{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。「[[神道五部書|倭姫命世記]]」の「神垂以祈祷為先冥加以正直以本」の語句に由来する。
=== 思想 === 垂加神道は、[[天照大神|天照大御神]]に対する信仰を大御神の子孫である天皇が統治する道を神道であると定義づけ、[[天皇]]への信仰、神儒の合一を主張し、尊王思想の高揚をもたらした{{Sfn|『必携日本史用語』}}。また、人間の「[[敬]]」を最も大切な徳分とし、敬を全うすれば天地と合一できる「天人唯一の理」を唱えた。この「敬」の実践行為とは「正直」であるとした{{Sfn|伊藤聡|2012}}。
=== 影響 === 垂加神道は、 その内容から、尊王思想の思想的バックボーンを形成することとなり、[[水戸学]]の[[尊王論]]や[[国粋主義]]思想に大きな影響を与えた{{Sfn|『必携日本史用語』}}。また、この系譜から[[竹内敬持|竹内式部]]や[[山県大弐]]のような熱烈な尊皇家が出た。[[浅見絅斎|浅見絧斎]]も儒教的大義名分論から尊王論を唱えた。やがてそこから[[日本主義]]＝[[国学]]の隆盛をもたらすこととなった{{要出典|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}}。
